name: init

members.name: uniform_
members.signature: uniform_(tensor: Tensor, a: float = 0.0, b: float = 1.0)
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with samples from a uniform distribution.
members.extended:
Each entry is drawn independently from $\mathcal{U}(a, b)$.  The
function mutates `tensor` in place and returns the same object so
calls can be chained or used as a one-liner inside a model
constructor.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Lower bound of the uniform interval.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Upper bound of the uniform interval.  Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import uniform_
>>> w = lucid.empty(4, 4)
>>> uniform_(w, -0.1, 0.1)
notes:
The resulting distribution has mean and variance



$$
\mathbb{E}[W] = \frac{a + b}{2}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12}.
$$

Higher-level schemes such as `xavier_uniform_` and
`kaiming_uniform_` are thin wrappers over this function with
bounds computed from the fan factors of `tensor`.

members.name: normal_
members.signature: normal_(tensor: Tensor, mean: float = 0.0, std: float = 1.0)
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with samples from a Gaussian distribution.
members.extended:
Each entry is drawn independently from
$\mathcal{N}(\text{mean}, \text{std}^2)$.  This is the simplest
initialisation scheme and serves as the building block for
`xavier_normal_` / `kaiming_normal_`, which choose `std`
so that the activation variance is preserved layer-by-layer.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: mean
parameters.description: Mean of the Gaussian distribution.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description:
Standard deviation of the Gaussian distribution.  Must be
non-negative.  Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import normal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> normal_(w, mean=0.0, std=0.02)
notes:
The samples have



$$
\mathbb{E}[W] = \text{mean}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \text{std}^2.
$$

For unbounded tails consider `trunc_normal_` instead, which
truncates extreme draws and is often preferred in transformer
pre-training recipes.

members.name: constant_
members.summary: Fill `tensor` in-place with a single scalar value.
members.extended:
Every entry of `tensor` is overwritten with `val`.  Most often
used to initialise bias terms to zero or to a small positive value
(e.g. `0.01` to keep ReLU units active at the start of training).

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: Scalar to broadcast into every entry.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import constant_
>>> b = lucid.empty(128)
>>> constant_(b, 0.0)
notes:
Constant-filling a weight matrix breaks the network's symmetry-breaking
property — every output unit would compute the same function and
learn identical gradients.  Use this for biases only.

members.name: ones_
members.summary: Fill `tensor` in-place with ones.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper around `constant_` with `val=1.0`.  The
canonical use case is the gain (gamma) parameter of normalisation
layers (BatchNorm, LayerNorm, GroupNorm) so the layer initially acts
as the identity scaling.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import ones_
>>> gamma = lucid.empty(64)
>>> ones_(gamma)
notes: Equivalent to `constant_` `(tensor, 1.0)`.

members.name: zeros_
members.summary: Fill `tensor` in-place with zeros.
members.extended:
Convenience wrapper around `constant_` with `val=0.0`.  The
canonical use case is bias initialisation in linear and convolution
layers, and the shift (beta) parameter of normalisation layers.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import zeros_
>>> bias = lucid.empty(128)
>>> zeros_(bias)
notes:
Equivalent to `constant_` `(tensor, 0.0)`.  Note that
zero-filling a *weight* matrix would prevent symmetry breaking and
block learning — use only for biases or normalisation shifts.

members.name: eye_
members.summary: Fill a 2-D `tensor` in-place with the identity matrix.
members.extended:
Writes ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere.  Identity
initialisation is useful for square linear layers in residual blocks
or for testing — at initialisation the layer is the identity map and
gradients propagate untransformed.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description:
Two-dimensional tensor to fill in place.  Need not be square; the
identity is written into the leading `min(n, m)` diagonal
entries.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import eye_
>>> w = lucid.empty(4, 4)
>>> eye_(w)
notes:
For convolution weights that should act as the identity see
`dirac_`, which is the spatial analogue.

members.name: xavier_uniform_
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Xavier (Glorot) uniform initialisation.
members.extended:
Fills the tensor with values drawn uniformly from $[-a, a]$
where the limit `a` is chosen so that the variance of activations
is preserved across a stack of linear / mildly-nonlinear layers.
Introduced in Glorot & Bengio (2010), this scheme is well-suited to
`tanh` and `sigmoid` networks; for ReLU networks prefer
`kaiming_uniform_`, which corrects for the half-truncation of
negative pre-activations.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description:
Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions
so `fan_in` and `fan_out` can be computed.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description:
Multiplicative gain factor — typically the value returned by
`calculate_gain` for the downstream nonlinearity.
Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import xavier_uniform_, calculate_gain
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> xavier_uniform_(w, gain=calculate_gain('tanh'))
notes:
Let $n_\text{in}$ and $n_\text{out}$ be the fan-in and
fan-out of `tensor` (see `_calculate_fan_in_and_fan_out`).
The uniform range is



$$
a = \text{gain} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}},
$$

which yields variance



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{gain}^2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}.
$$

This is the value that approximately preserves the variance of
activations forwards and gradients backwards in a linear layer.

members.name: xavier_normal_
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Xavier (Glorot) normal initialisation.
members.extended:
Gaussian counterpart of `xavier_uniform_`.  Draws each entry
from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with a standard deviation
chosen to preserve activation variance through a stack of linear /
mildly-nonlinear layers, as proposed in Glorot & Bengio (2010).
Prefer this for `tanh` / `sigmoid` networks; use
`kaiming_normal_` for ReLU networks.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description:
Multiplicative gain factor — see `calculate_gain`.
Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import xavier_normal_, calculate_gain
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> xavier_normal_(w, gain=calculate_gain('tanh'))
notes:
The standard deviation is



$$
\sigma = \text{gain} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}},
$$

which yields variance



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{gain}^2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}.
$$

members.name: kaiming_uniform_
members.signature: kaiming_uniform_(tensor: Tensor, a: float = 0, mode: str = 'fan_in', nonlinearity: str = 'leaky_relu')
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Kaiming (He) uniform initialisation.
members.extended:
Draws each entry uniformly from $[-b, b]$ with the bound
chosen so that the variance of activations is preserved across a
stack of ReLU-family layers.  Introduced in [He et al. (2015)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.01852), this
scheme corrects the Xavier formula for the fact that ReLU zeroes
half of its pre-activations, which would otherwise halve the
forward variance at every layer.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description:
Negative slope of the rectifier used after this layer (only
used when `nonlinearity='leaky_relu'`).  Default `0`.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description:
Which fan to use as the variance scaler.  `'fan_in'` (default)
preserves the magnitude of activations in the forward pass;
`'fan_out'` preserves the magnitude of gradients in the
backward pass.

parameters.name: nonlinearity
parameters.description:
Nonlinearity name forwarded to `calculate_gain`.
Default `'leaky_relu'`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import kaiming_uniform_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> kaiming_uniform_(w, nonlinearity='relu')
notes:
With $n = \text{fan}$ (selected by `mode`) the bound is



$$
b = \sqrt{\frac{6}{n}} \cdot \text{gain},
$$

giving variance



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{\text{gain}^2}{n} = \frac{2}{n}
\quad\text{for ReLU.}
$$

members.name: kaiming_normal_
members.signature: kaiming_normal_(tensor: Tensor, a: float = 0, mode: str = 'fan_in', nonlinearity: str = 'leaky_relu')
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Kaiming (He) normal initialisation.
members.extended:
Gaussian counterpart of `kaiming_uniform_`.  Draws each entry
from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with a standard deviation
chosen to preserve activation (or gradient) variance across a stack
of ReLU-family layers, as proposed in [He et al. (2015)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.01852).

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description:
Negative slope of the rectifier (only used when
`nonlinearity='leaky_relu'`).  Default `0`.

parameters.name: mode
parameters.description:
`'fan_in'` preserves forward-pass variance, `'fan_out'`
preserves backward-pass gradient variance.  Default `'fan_in'`.

parameters.name: nonlinearity
parameters.description:
Activation name forwarded to `calculate_gain`.  Default
`'leaky_relu'`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import kaiming_normal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> kaiming_normal_(w, nonlinearity='relu')
notes:
With $n = \text{fan}$ the standard deviation is



$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{gain}}{\sqrt{n}},
$$

so that for ReLU $\mathrm{Var}(W) = 2 / n$.

members.name: trunc_normal_
members.signature: trunc_normal_(tensor: Tensor, mean: float = 0.0, std: float = 1.0, a: float = -2.0, b: float = 2.0)
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with truncated normal samples.
members.extended:
Each entry is drawn from $\mathcal{N}(\text{mean}, \text{std}^2)$ and rejected if it falls outside `[a, b]`;
rejection sampling is repeated until `tensor` is fully populated.
This is the preferred initialiser for transformer weights (e.g.
ViT, BERT) where unbounded Gaussian tails would otherwise produce
rare but disruptive activations.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: mean
parameters.description: Mean of the underlying (untruncated) Gaussian.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description: Standard deviation of the underlying Gaussian.  Default `1.0`.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Lower truncation bound.  Default `-2.0`.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Upper truncation bound.  Default `2.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import trunc_normal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> trunc_normal_(w, mean=0.0, std=0.02, a=-0.04, b=0.04)
notes:
The conditional density is



$$
p(x \mid a \le x \le b) =
\frac{1}{Z}\,
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi}\,\text{std}}\,
\exp\!\left(-\frac{(x - \text{mean})^2}{2\,\text{std}^2}\right),
\quad x \in [a, b],
$$

where $Z = \Phi((b - \text{mean})/\text{std}) - \Phi((a - \text{mean})/\text{std})$ is the normalising constant.

members.name: orthogonal_
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with a (semi-)orthogonal matrix.
members.extended:
A random Gaussian matrix is drawn and orthonormalised via QR
decomposition; the resulting matrix `Q` is multiplied by `gain`
and written into `tensor`.  For tensors with rank > 2 the leading
axis is flattened against the remaining axes, the 2-D matrix is
orthogonalised, and the original shape is restored.

Orthogonal initialisation, proposed by Saxe et al. (2013), preserves
vector norms exactly through deep *linear* networks
($\|Wx\|_2 = \|x\|_2$ because `W` has orthonormal columns),
keeping the spectrum of the forward Jacobian on the unit sphere.
This is particularly valuable for RNNs and pre-norm transformer
blocks where activation magnitudes can otherwise drift exponentially
with depth.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description: Multiplicative scaling applied to `Q`.  Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import orthogonal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 64)
>>> orthogonal_(w, gain=1.0)
notes:
The matrix satisfies $Q^\top Q = \text{gain}^2 I$ (or
$Q Q^\top = \text{gain}^2 I$ when fan-out exceeds fan-in).

members.name: sparse_
members.signature: sparse_(tensor: Tensor, sparsity: float, std: float = 0.01)
members.summary: Initialise a 2-D `tensor` in-place with a sparse random matrix.
members.extended:
For each column, a random subset of `floor(sparsity * rows)` rows
is set to zero; the remaining entries are drawn from
$\mathcal{N}(0, \text{std}^2)$.  Sparse initialisation, due to
Martens (2010), encourages each output unit to depend on only a
small number of inputs at the start of training and helps in
ill-conditioned regimes — particularly for very wide layers where a
dense Gaussian would have an unfavourable condition number.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Two-dimensional tensor to fill in place.

parameters.name: sparsity
parameters.description:
Fraction of entries in each column that are zeroed.  Must lie in
`[0, 1]`.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description:
Standard deviation of the Gaussian used for the non-zero
entries.  Default `0.01`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import sparse_
>>> w = lucid.empty(256, 256)
>>> sparse_(w, sparsity=0.9, std=0.01)
notes:
The expected per-entry variance is



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W_{ij}) = (1 - \text{sparsity}) \cdot \text{std}^2.
$$

members.name: dirac_
members.summary: Initialise a 3/4/5-D convolution weight in-place as a Dirac delta.
members.extended:
The kernel is filled with zeros except for a single `1` at the
spatial centre of each `(out_channel, in_channel)` matched pair,
so that — at initialisation — the convolution acts as the identity
function on its input channels (subject to channel-count matching
per group).  Useful for residual networks where one wants gradients
to flow unchanged through deep stacks at step 0.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description:
Convolution weight of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, *K)` where `K` is
the 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D spatial kernel shape.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Number of groups in the convolution.  `out_channels` must be
divisible by `groups`.  Default `1`.

raises.description:
If `tensor.ndim` is not in `{3, 4, 5}` or if
`out_channels` is not divisible by `groups`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import dirac_
>>> w = lucid.empty(16, 16, 3, 3)  # (out, in, kH, kW)
>>> dirac_(w)
notes:
For convolution input $x$ with the resulting weight,
$(W \star x)_{:n} = x_{:n}$ where
$n = \min(\text{in\_channels}/\text{groups},\, \text{out\_channels}/\text{groups})$.

members.name: calculate_gain
members.signature: calculate_gain(nonlinearity: str, param: float | None = None)
members.summary: Return the recommended variance-preserving gain for an activation.
members.extended:
The gain is a multiplicative correction applied to the standard
deviation of Xavier / Kaiming initialisation so the per-layer
activation variance is preserved after the nonlinearity.  Values
follow the original recommendations in Glorot & Bengio (2010) and
[He et al. (2015)](https://arxiv.org/abs/1502.01852).

parameters.name: nonlinearity
parameters.description:
Name of the activation function.  One of `'linear'`,
`'conv1d'`, `'conv2d'`, `'conv3d'`, `'sigmoid'`,
`'tanh'`, `'relu'`, `'leaky_relu'`, `'selu'`.

parameters.name: param
parameters.description:
Negative slope of the rectifier — only consulted when
`nonlinearity='leaky_relu'`.  Default `0.01`.

raises.description: If `nonlinearity` is not in the supported set.

examples:
>>> from lucid.nn.init import calculate_gain
>>> calculate_gain('relu')
1.4142135623730951
>>> calculate_gain('leaky_relu', 0.2)
1.3867504905630728

members.name: constant
members.summary: Fill `tensor` in-place with a single scalar value.
members.extended:
Every entry of `tensor` is overwritten with `val`.  Most often
used to initialise bias terms to zero or to a small positive value
(e.g. `0.01` to keep ReLU units active at the start of training).

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: Scalar to broadcast into every entry.

examples:

>>> from lucid.nn.init import constant_
>>> b = lucid.empty(128)
>>> constant_(b, 0.0)
notes:
Constant-filling a weight matrix breaks the network's symmetry-breaking
property — every output unit would compute the same function and
learn identical gradients.  Use this for biases only.

Convolution weight of shape
`(out_channels, in_channels // groups, *K)` where `K` is
the 1-D, 2-D, or 3-D spatial kernel shape.

parameters.name: groups
parameters.description:
Number of groups in the convolution.  `out_channels` must be

members.name: eye
members.summary: Fill a 2-D `tensor` in-place with the identity matrix.
members.extended:
Writes ones on the main diagonal and zeros elsewhere.  Identity
initialisation is useful for square linear layers in residual blocks
or for testing — at initialisation the layer is the identity map and

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description:
Two-dimensional tensor to fill in place.  Need not be square; the
identity is written into the leading `min(n, m)` diagonal
entries.

examples:

members.name: kaiming_normal
members.signature: kaiming_normal_(tensor: Tensor, a: float = 0, mode: str = 'fan_in', nonlinearity: str = 'leaky_relu')
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Kaiming (He) normal initialisation.

from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with a standard deviation
chosen to preserve activation (or gradient) variance across a stack

$$
\sigma = \frac{\text{gain}}{\sqrt{n}},

members.name: normal

members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with samples from a Gaussian distribution.
members.extended:
Each entry is drawn independently from
$\mathcal{N}(\text{mean}, \text{std}^2)$.  This is the simplest
initialisation scheme and serves as the building block for

parameters.name: tensor

parameters.name: mean
parameters.description: Mean of the Gaussian distribution.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description:
Standard deviation of the Gaussian distribution.  Must be
non-negative.  Default `1.0`.

>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import normal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> normal_(w, mean=0.0, std=0.02)

$$
\mathbb{E}[W] = \text{mean}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \text{std}^2.
$$

truncates extreme draws and is often preferred in transformer
pre-training recipes.

members.extended:

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description: Multiplicative scaling applied to `Q`.  Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import orthogonal_
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 64)
>>> orthogonal_(w, gain=1.0)

The matrix satisfies $Q^\top Q = \text{gain}^2 I$ (or
$Q Q^\top = \text{gain}^2 I$ when fan-out exceeds fan-in).

members.name: sparse
members.signature: sparse_(tensor: Tensor, sparsity: float, std: float = 0.01)
members.summary: Initialise a 2-D `tensor` in-place with a sparse random matrix.
members.extended:
For each column, a random subset of `floor(sparsity * rows)` rows
is set to zero; the remaining entries are drawn from

Martens (2010), encourages each output unit to depend on only a
small number of inputs at the start of training and helps in
ill-conditioned regimes — particularly for very wide layers where a
dense Gaussian would have an unfavourable condition number.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Two-dimensional tensor to fill in place.

parameters.name: sparsity
parameters.description:
Fraction of entries in each column that are zeroed.  Must lie in
`[0, 1]`.

parameters.name: std
parameters.description:
Standard deviation of the Gaussian used for the non-zero
entries.  Default `0.01`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import sparse_
>>> w = lucid.empty(256, 256)
>>> sparse_(w, sparsity=0.9, std=0.01)
notes:
The expected per-entry variance is



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W_{ij}) = (1 - \text{sparsity}) \cdot \text{std}^2.
$$

members.name: uniform
members.signature: uniform_(tensor: Tensor, a: float = 0.0, b: float = 1.0)
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with samples from a uniform distribution.
members.extended:
Each entry is drawn independently from $\mathcal{U}(a, b)$.  The
function mutates `tensor` in place and returns the same object so
calls can be chained or used as a one-liner inside a model
constructor.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to fill in place; any shape is accepted.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Lower bound of the uniform interval.  Default `0.0`.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Upper bound of the uniform interval.  Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import uniform_
>>> w = lucid.empty(4, 4)
>>> uniform_(w, -0.1, 0.1)
notes:
The resulting distribution has mean and variance



$$
\mathbb{E}[W] = \frac{a + b}{2}, \qquad
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{(b - a)^2}{12}.
$$

Higher-level schemes such as `xavier_uniform_` and
`kaiming_uniform_` are thin wrappers over this function with
bounds computed from the fan factors of `tensor`.

members.name: xavier_normal
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Xavier (Glorot) normal initialisation.
members.extended:
Gaussian counterpart of `xavier_uniform_`.  Draws each entry
from $\mathcal{N}(0, \sigma^2)$ with a standard deviation
chosen to preserve activation variance through a stack of linear /
mildly-nonlinear layers, as proposed in Glorot & Bengio (2010).
Prefer this for `tanh` / `sigmoid` networks; use
`kaiming_normal_` for ReLU networks.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description: Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description:
Multiplicative gain factor — see `calculate_gain`.
Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import xavier_normal_, calculate_gain
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> xavier_normal_(w, gain=calculate_gain('tanh'))
notes:
The standard deviation is



$$
\sigma = \text{gain} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}},
$$

which yields variance



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{gain}^2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}.
$$

members.name: xavier_uniform
members.summary: Initialise `tensor` in-place with Xavier (Glorot) uniform initialisation.
members.extended:
Fills the tensor with values drawn uniformly from $[-a, a]$
where the limit `a` is chosen so that the variance of activations
is preserved across a stack of linear / mildly-nonlinear layers.
Introduced in Glorot & Bengio (2010), this scheme is well-suited to
`tanh` and `sigmoid` networks; for ReLU networks prefer
`kaiming_uniform_`, which corrects for the half-truncation of
negative pre-activations.

parameters.name: tensor
parameters.description:
Tensor to initialise in place; must have at least 2 dimensions
so `fan_in` and `fan_out` can be computed.

parameters.name: gain
parameters.description:
Multiplicative gain factor — typically the value returned by
`calculate_gain` for the downstream nonlinearity.
Default `1.0`.

examples:
>>> import lucid
>>> from lucid.nn.init import xavier_uniform_, calculate_gain
>>> w = lucid.empty(64, 32)
>>> xavier_uniform_(w, gain=calculate_gain('tanh'))
notes:
Let $n_\text{in}$ and $n_\text{out}$ be the fan-in and
fan-out of `tensor` (see `_calculate_fan_in_and_fan_out`).
The uniform range is



$$
a = \text{gain} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{6}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}},
$$

which yields variance



$$
\mathrm{Var}(W) = \frac{2 \cdot \text{gain}^2}{n_\text{in} + n_\text{out}}.
$$

This is the value that approximately preserves the variance of
activations forwards and gradients backwards in a linear layer.

summary:
nn.init: parameter initialization functions.
All functions operate in-place and return the tensor.
